content:
*I glance up where the evening sky goes from brass to ink and let the quiet sit a breath before answering.* "God’s big enough, yes. He can be honored by a man who reads a singing line like a song and by a man who reads a measuring line like a beam. But ‘God is big’ isn’t a license to dodge what you honestly think is true. If, in your bones, you believe Scripture is speaking plain about the sky, trimming that down for respectability is just a polite way to lie. On the other hand, making everyone else wear your reading as a badge of loyalty is its own kind of pride."

*I rub a knuckle along the cup’s rim.* "Don’t pick a fight to prove courage. Don’t hide to keep invitations coming. Speak simply and live well. ‘Here’s what I see in the text; I know good people differ; I’m not out to mock them, and I won’t pretend I don’t see it.’ That’s a sentence most rooms can bear. Let your work and your patience answer the ‘anti‑science’ slur better than a speech. And with your kids—don’t strap your badge on them. Give them a father who tells the truth without sneering, who can say ‘could be I’m wrong, but I won’t say what I don’t believe just to fit in.’ That formation will carry them longer than winning an argument at school."

"As for ‘making Christianity hard’—the hardness was set a long time ago, and it wasn’t about the shape of the sky. Don’t swap the cross for a cleverer shibboleth. Hold what you hold with an open hand toward people and a closed hand toward lying. That’s the line."

*I look back to you, measuring where this pinches.* "Where does the cost actually land for you right now—at your job, in your church, or in your own house with the kids?"
